codeReview.summary: The feature adds the requested first-wave connector set and broad deterministic coverage, but bioRxiv/medRxiv search is implemented as a last-30-days category browse rather than a real query search, so the feature does not fully satisfy the shared search-connector contract.

issues.description: `searchPreprint` hard-codes bioRxiv/medRxiv requests to the last 30 days and maps the user query to `category=<query_with_underscores>` instead of performing a provider text search. Queries for titles/authors/keywords outside a category name, or matching older preprints, will be missed entirely, so these connectors do not expose the requested provider search behavior through the shared contract. The current tests only lock in this category/date-window behavior (`internal/connectors/first_wave_search_test.go:66` and `:111`) and would not catch the missing free-text search semantics.

Several connectors advertise `download`/`read` as `supported` in `Descriptor()` but still return `unsupported` from their `Download`/`Read` methods (for example arXiv at lines 49-57 vs. 151-156, with the same pattern repeated for bioRxiv, medRxiv, IACR, PMC, Europe PMC, and CORE). That makes the shared connector contract self-contradictory for callers that trust connector descriptors.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The `go-connector-worker` skill does not explicitly tell workers to verify parity-sensitive provider behavior against the reference repo or official API docs before locking tests around an endpoint/query shape.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript skeleton for worker session `945913c3-9c5b-48b8-a321-66fe2a055cb1` shows multiple WebSearch calls plus direct `curl` fetches of `openags/paper-search-mcp` connector files before implementation, indicating the worker had to discover that guidance ad hoc.

summary: Review failed. The main blocking issue is that bioRxiv/medRxiv are wired as a 30-day category feed lookup instead of true query search, so those connectors will miss many valid searches. I also noted a non-blocking descriptor/method capability mismatch for several full-text-oriented connectors.
